title: Depthwise Convolutions: One Filter Per Channel
concept: A depthwise convolution gives every input channel its own private spatial filter instead of one filter reading across all channels at once — the channels stay separate, and the weight count drops dramatically.

exercises.prompt: Recap: dilated convolutions grow a network's receptive field without doing what?

options.text: Shrinking the feature map's spatial resolution
Changing the filter's sample point spacing

exercises.explain: Right — dilation trades weight count for resolution safety, growing reach without shrinking the map. Now let's meet a totally different way to cut cost: give each channel its own filter.

cards.title: One head chef vs. one cook per ingredient
cards.body: A standard convolution filter is like a head chef who tastes every single ingredient on the counter together to decide what to cook next. A depthwise convolution instead hires one dedicated cook per ingredient — each cook only ever touches their one ingredient, never tasting the others.

cards.title: What is a depthwise convolution?
cards.body: A depthwise convolution applies one small spatial filter (say, 3x3) to each input channel completely independently. With C input channels, you get C separate 3x3 filters — one per channel — and the output also has exactly C channels. No summing or blending across channels ever happens, unlike a standard convolution, where each output value is a sum across every input channel.

cards.title: Separate lanes, never merging
cards.body: Think of it as channels running down separate highway lanes. A standard convolution has on-ramps merging every lane into one before producing an output. A depthwise convolution keeps every lane running in its own dedicated lane, filtered on its own, with zero merging.

cards.title: 8 channels, two very different weight counts
cards.body: A standard 3x3 convolution over 8 input channels producing 8 output channels needs 8 filters, each reading all 8 channels: 3×3×8×8 = 576 weights. A depthwise version instead gives each of the 8 channels its own private 3x3 filter: 8 × (3×3) = 72 weights total. Same 8 channels in, same 8 channels out — but 8x fewer weights, because no filter ever reads more than its one assigned channel.

cards.title: Depthwise doesn't change channel count
cards.body: It's tempting to think a "simpler" filter must also shrink the number of channels, the way Unit 100's 1x1 convs are often used to squeeze channel depth. It doesn't — depthwise convolution always outputs exactly as many channels as it takes in, since it's strictly one filter per channel. It filters spatially; it never fattens or slims channel depth.

cards.title: Standard conv vs. depthwise conv
cards.body: Same channels in, very different wiring:

left.text:
One filter reads
ALL channels at once
mixes them into 1 output

right.text:
One filter per channel
channels stay separate
no mixing at all

exercises.prompt: A depthwise convolution processes an 8-channel input. How many separate 3x3 filters does it use, and how many output channels result?

options.text: 8 filters (one per channel), producing 8 output channels
1 combined filter reading all 8 channels, producing 1 output channel
8 filters, but only 1 output channel because they're summed together
64 filters, covering every possible input-output channel pair

exercises.explain: Correct — depthwise means exactly one filter per channel, and every channel stays separate all the way through to the output.

exercises.prompt: True or false: a depthwise convolution mixes information across channels, just like a standard convolution does.
exercises.explain: False — depthwise convolutions keep every channel completely separate. No cross-channel mixing happens at this step.

exercises.sentence: Each channel gets its own small filter, so a depthwise convolution's output channel count always equals its ___ channel count.
exercises.explain: One filter per input channel means the output always matches the input channel count exactly — depthwise never changes channel depth.

exercises.prompt: Which statement correctly describes a depthwise convolution applied to a 16-channel feature map with 3x3 filters?

options.text: 16 separate 3x3 filters, one per channel, producing 16 output channels with no cross-channel mixing
One 3x3x16 filter that reads all 16 channels at once to build a single output channel
A single 1x1 filter that blends the 16 channels down to 4

exercises.explain: Right — depthwise convolution assigns one filter per channel and keeps all 16 channels separate throughout.

exercises.prompt: A standard 3x3 convolution over 16 input channels producing 16 output channels needs 3×3×16×16 = 2,304 weights. Replace it with a depthwise 3x3 convolution instead (one filter per input channel, no mixing). How many weights does it need?

options.text: 3×3×16 = 144 weights, since each of the 16 channels only needs its own small filter
2,304 weights — the same as the standard convolution
3×3 = 9 weights total, shared across all 16 channels

exercises.explain: Correct — 144 weights, because each filter only ever reads its own channel instead of all 16. Depthwise convs cut weight count roughly by a factor of the channel count. Next: how those 16 separate, un-mixed channels get combined back together.
